name: Foundation/ms-custom-headers
description: Custom response header conformance tests ported from Microsoft.Health.Fhir.Shared.Tests.E2E Rest/CustomHeadersTests.cs. Covers the X-Request-Id/X-Correlation-Id correlation contract and the CosmosDB-specific continuation-token-size request header. The engine has no runtime random-value generation (per the constraint that ${variable} substitution only resolves values already captured from fixtures/prior responses), so the source test's per-run Guid.NewGuid() request id is replaced with a fixed literal id, which is sufficient to prove the correlation behavior. The original 'no X-Request-Id sent -> server must not echo X-Correlation-Id' assertion is skipped: the source test itself only conditionally asserts this because a reverse proxy may inject its own X-Request-Id ahead of the FHIR server, making the outcome environment-dependent and not reliably expressible as a single assertion. The CosmosDB continuation-token-size tests are warningOnly because that header is only meaningfully validated by CosmosDB-backed deployments; other datastores are expected to ignore it and return success regardless of value.

test.name: X-Request-Id sent by the client is echoed back as X-Correlation-Id, not as X-Request-Id
test.description: Sending a client-supplied X-Request-Id must result in the server returning that same value in X-Correlation-Id, while the server's own X-Request-Id response header must remain distinct from the client-supplied value.

operation.description: Search Patient with a client-supplied X-Request-Id header

assert.description: X-Correlation-Id response header must equal the client-supplied X-Request-Id value
Server's own X-Request-Id response header must not contain the client-supplied value

test.name: requests without a client X-Request-Id still receive a server-generated X-Request-Id
test.description: When no X-Request-Id is supplied by the client, the server must still return HTTP 200 with a non-empty, server-generated X-Request-Id response header. The source test's additional assertion that X-Correlation-Id is absent is skipped here: the source test only checks that conditionally, since a reverse proxy in front of the server may inject its own X-Request-Id, making that outcome environment-dependent.

operation.description: Search Patient without any client-supplied request id header

assert.description: X-Request-Id response header must be present and non-empty

test.name: a valid CosmosDB continuation token size limit header does not break the request
test.description: A recognized, in-range continuation token size (1-3 Kb; '2' is representative of the equivalent '1'/'3' rows) must not cause a failure on any backend: CosmosDB-backed servers honor it, and other datastores are expected to ignore an unrecognized header and still succeed.

operation.description: Search Patient with a valid CosmosDB continuation token size limit header

assert.description: Request must succeed with HTTP 200 regardless of backing datastore

test.name: an invalid CosmosDB continuation token size limit header is rejected by CosmosDB-backed servers
test.description: CosmosDB-backed servers must return HTTP 400 for a malformed token size ('invalid'; representative of the non-numeric/overflow/decimal rows) or an out-of-range numeric token size ('5'; representative of out-of-range rows like '-1'). Both assertions are warningOnly because this validation is CosmosDB-specific; other datastores are expected to ignore the header and return 200 instead.

operation.description: Search Patient with a malformed (non-numeric) continuation token size limit header

assert.description: CosmosDB-backed servers must return HTTP 400 for a malformed token size (warningOnly: other datastores ignore this header)

operation.description: Search Patient with an out-of-range (too large) continuation token size limit header

assert.description: CosmosDB-backed servers must return HTTP 400 for an out-of-range token size (warningOnly: other datastores ignore this header)
